comment.body:
where MMs shfist from short skew positioning to long skew.
i just explained the spot/vol beta above, read that.
but i will write about these concepts more deeply, from the very basics to the advanced things.

in short: above zero speed, MMs are longer skew: longer spot/vol beta, shorter upside gamma/longer downside gamma
below zero speed, MMs are shorter skew: shorter spot/vol beta, longer upside gamma/shorter downside gamma.

so thats kind of a balance, where MMs gamma exposure shifts. above they have more gamma to the downside thant to the upside, and vice versa

comment.body:
think this way: supportive doesn't necessary means "pushing up". 
from this perspective short speed is more "supportive" with its fat right-tail risk.

but supportive means stabilizing, more liqudiity, vol can compress.
so maybe the price is pinned, but stabile ins a huge positive gamma territory.
vanna has a complex effect, it is usually have a longer-time frame effect, but vanna is not easy, that will get an indivisual looong post to explain.

charm has very little effect usually... it comes into play at the end of the day. and also it is increased if vol is high, especially around the 25 delta strikes.

most of the times it is gamma. gamma has an inverse relationship with volatility, and it must be hedged instantly

comment.body:
Hi Alma, thank you for the intelligent analysis.
I have a question: How can we tell which of the three greeks- speed, vanna, charm- has the greatest impact to the spot price movement?

Let's say we are in the middle of a "positive speed & positive vanna & negative charm" area, and the spot price is going upwards. Here is my understanding:
1. Positive speed has a downward effect because spot up→gamma up→MMs buy less or sell more delta
2. Positive vanna has an upward effect because spot up→vol down→delta down→MMs buy delta
3. Negative charm has an upward effect because time passes→delta down→MMs buy delta

But I'm not sure which one is dominating. I assume charm matters a lot right before the expiration, but not sure about other cases.

comment.body:
Hi Alma, I do have some questions to properly decode your text :)
(1) You wrote "MMs have a bull put spread pattern" (or similar for butterfly). Do I interpret correctly that it means that the net gamma profile resembles the gamma profile of a typical bull put spread, so it can be very roughly approximated as if the MM is long put strike X + short put strike <larger than X> - resp. the customers' position is roughly the opposite (long put, below short put). Mapping it to the option strategy standards is more a mental model that allows to differentiate between gamma setups.
comment.raw_body:

comment.body: If MM has positive gamma (costumers short gamma) the market is slow because MM buy/sell againts the movement. If gamma is negative moviments are sharp because MM supports the movement

comment.body: Alma, idk if this is the right place to ask this or if this is even apart of your process, but what is the significance of gamma and delta being positive or negative? comment.body:
whenever you long an option, you long the gamma 
(long = positive
short = negative)

MM takes the opposite of the trade. 
so he will be short gamma.

now imagine that you long an 50 delta call, with a gamma of lets say 2
MM will have  -50 delta call with gamma of -2

he has -50 delta, so he buys 50 shres of stock (1 delta assets, as we call them, futures, stocks.. but not true for VIX futs!!!)
Now if spot moves up by 1 pt, you add the gamma to the delta like: (-50) + (-2) = -52
so the MM became shorter delta, he has to buy 2 more shares of stocks
so he buys into the direction.

if the spot moves down, you simply extract the gamma values from the delta:
(-50) - (-2) = -50 +2 = -48
so now, MM only need to have 48 shares of stock, so he will sell 2 shares. 
So again, he is hedging into the price direction
comment.raw_body:
whenever you long an option, you long the gamma 
(long = positive
short = negative)

MM takes the opposite of the trade. 
so he will be short gamma.

now imagine that you long an 50 delta call, with a gamma of lets say 2
MM will have  -50 delta call with gamma of -2

he has -50 delta, so he buys 50 shres of stock (1 delta assets, as we call them, futures, stocks.. but not true for VIX futs!!!)
Now if spot moves up by 1 pt, you add the gamma to the delta like: (-50) + (-2) = -52
so the MM became shorter delta, he has to buy 2 more shares of stocks
so he buys into the direction.

if the spot moves down, you simply extract the gamma values from the delta:
(-50) - (-2) = -50 +2 = -48
so now, MM only need to have 48 shares of stock, so he will sell 2 shares. 
So again, he is hedging into the price direction

comment.body:
what if if you would have sold the same 50 delta call with gamma of 2?
now your position would be -50 delta and -2 gamma
and the MM would long 50 delta and 2 gamma.
so he sells 50 shares of stocks.

If spot moves up, his exposure becomes: 50+2 = 52
meaning, he has to sell 2 more. 
In this case, he hedge against the price action. 

if spot moves down, his exposure becomes: 50-2 = 48
meaning, he has to buyback 2.
again, hedges against the price movement.
selling rips and buying dips.

this compresses the ranges.
